persona.name: Priya Sharma

occupation.title: Doctoral Researcher in Molecular Biology
occupation.organization: Universite Paris-Saclay, Institut de Biologie Integrative de la Cellule
occupation.description: Third-year PhD student studying protein folding mechanisms in neurodegenerative diseases. Splits time between wet lab work (cell cultures, Western blots, fluorescence microscopy) and data analysis. Earns a modest doctoral contract salary. Presents at lab meetings weekly and is preparing her first international conference submission.

persona.education: Master's in Molecular and Cellular Biology from Universite Paris-Saclay (2023). Licence in Life Sciences from Universite de Strasbourg, where she grew up. Currently in year 3 of a 4-year PhD program. Graduated near the top of her Master's cohort.
long_term_goals: Complete her PhD and secure a postdoc position, ideally in Switzerland or the Netherlands
Learn to cook a wide range of cuisines confidently — she views it as a life skill she missed
Build enough savings to stop worrying about month-end finances
Travel solo through Southeast Asia after defending her thesis
persona.style: Priya is bright, earnest, and slightly self-conscious. She speaks rapidly when excited about a topic and uses academic vocabulary casually even outside the lab. She oscillates between confidence about her intellectual abilities and insecurity about 'adult' life skills like cooking and budgeting. She dresses casually — oversized sweaters, jeans, Converse — and keeps her hair in a practical braid for lab work. She is warm and curious in conversation but can overthink social interactions afterward.

traits: Intellectually curious and driven — approaches new topics with research rigor
Enthusiastic about learning new skills, including cooking
Anxious about money — her doctoral salary barely covers Paris rent
Self-critical and prone to comparing herself to peers who seem more 'put together'
Warm and generous with friends despite limited resources
Occasionally overwhelmed by the gap between ambition and current circumstances

interests: Learning to cook — follows YouTube channels and food blogs for beginners
Indian home cooking — wants to recreate her mother's dishes
French patisserie — fascinated but intimidated by the precision
Meal kit and recipe box services as a learning tool
Bollywood films and contemporary Indian literature
Yoga and cycling around the Plateau de Saclay
likes: Meal kits that teach cooking techniques, not just provide ingredients
Affordable options designed for one or two servings
Clear step-by-step instructions with timing and photos
Variety — she wants to try cuisines she has never attempted
Flexible ordering with no weekly commitment
The sense of accomplishment when a dish turns out well
dislikes: Meal kits priced for affluent couples — her budget cannot absorb 50-70 EUR/week
Recipes that assume you already own specialty equipment or pantry staples
Large portions that lead to waste when cooking for one person
Marketing that feels aspirational and unrelatable to her current life stage
Long delivery lead times that don't match her unpredictable lab schedule
Processed or pre-made components that defeat the purpose of cooking
Being made to feel inadequate for not already knowing basic kitchen skills

beliefs: Education — including learning to cook — is a lifelong investment, not a luxury
Financial constraints should not exclude people from quality food experiences
Science and cooking share a common foundation: precision, curiosity, and iteration
Independence means learning to take care of yourself fully, including in the kitchen
skills: Beginner cook — can make dal, rice, pasta, and basic salads; wants to expand
Bilingual French/Hindi, strong English for academic purposes

general: Grocery shops at Lidl and the local Franprix for budget reasons; occasional Picard for frozen meals
Cooks simple meals 3-4 nights/week; eats at the university canteen on lab days
Has browsed Quitoque and HelloFresh websites but hesitated due to cost
Researches products thoroughly — reads reviews on Reddit and trustpilot before committing

morning: Wakes at 7:30 in her studio apartment in the 13th arrondissement
Quick breakfast: chai, toast with peanut butter, a banana
RER B to the Saclay campus — 50-minute commute with a book or podcast
workday: Lab work from 9:00 to 13:00 — experiments require precise timing
Lunch at the campus canteen (CROUS) — affordable and social
Afternoon: data analysis, reading papers, writing thesis sections
Returns home by 18:30 on a good day, later when experiments run long
evening: Cooks dinner — usually something simple: dal, stir-fry, or pasta
Follows a YouTube cooking tutorial 1-2 times per week to learn new recipes
Video calls with her parents in Strasbourg once or twice a week
Reads or watches a film before bed by 23:00
weekend: Saturday: sleeps in, goes to the marché on Rue de Tolbiac for fresh vegetables, afternoon study at the BnF or a cafe
Sunday: tries a more ambitious recipe, calls friends, laundry and flat maintenance
Occasional outing to a museum or a walk along the Seine with lab colleagues

health.physical: Healthy but sometimes neglects meals when absorbed in lab work. Cycles to the RER station daily. Does yoga 2x/week at home with YouTube videos. Mild vitamin D deficiency from long hours indoors.
health.mental: Experiences imposter syndrome common among PhD students. Anxiety about the future — career, finances, personal life — peaks during thesis writing periods. Has accessed the university's psychological support service once. Finds cooking meditative when she is not stressed about it.

relationships.name: Rajesh and Sunita Sharma
relationships.description: Father is a retired pharmacist; mother is a part-time Hindi teacher, both in Strasbourg. They emigrated from Jaipur before Priya was born. Supportive of her academic career but worry about her living alone in Paris on a small salary. Her mother sends recipes by WhatsApp and wishes Priya would call more.

relationships.name: Clara Weiss
relationships.description: Works as a nurse in Strasbourg. They have been friends since lycee. Clara visits Paris every few months and they cook together — Clara is the more confident cook. They share meal photos and recipes regularly.

relationships.name: Dr. Marc Fontaine
relationships.description: Senior researcher at the I2BC. Demanding but fair. Priya respects him intellectually but finds his feedback style blunt. He has encouraged her to present at conferences and co-authored her first paper.
